author: śrīla saccidānanda bhaktibinoda ṭhākura
verses:
sthāyibhāvāviṣṭa⋅citta, pāiyā vibhāvavitta,
udbhaṭa bhāvete āpanāra
prāṇa-vṛtte nyāsa kare, prāṇa sei nyāsabhare,
deha⋅prati vikṛti cālāya

vaivarṇya, romāñca, sveda, stambha, kampa, svara-bheda,
pralayāśru e aṣṭavikāra
sañcārī je bhāvacaya, harṣāmarṣa āra bhaya,
viṣāda, vismayādi tā’ra

pravṛttikāraṇa haya, līlākāle rase laya,
āpane karāya anukṣaṇa
dhūmāyitā, ujjvalitā, dīptā āra se uddīpta,
ei cāri avasthā lakṣaṇa

jā’ra jei adhikāra, sāttvika vikāra tā’ra,
se lakṣaṇe haya ta’ udaya
mahābhāva⋅daśā jathā, su⋅uddīpta bhāva tathā,
anāyāse sulakṣita haya
en_translation:
A mind absorbed in the permanent sentiment, upon receiving stimuli, spontaneously places itself in an extraordinary emotional state within the life-airs. The life-airs, thus filled, then cause transformations in the body.

Discoloration, horripilation, perspiration, stupefaction, trembling, change of voice, and tears—these are the eight transformations. Transient emotions like joy, indignation, fear, despondency, wonder, and others also arise.

They become the cause of actions, and during the divine pastimes, they merge into transcendental mellows, constantly manifesting themselves. Obscured, glowing, blazing, and fully manifest—these are the symptoms of the four stages.

According to one's qualification, their physical transformations indeed manifest with those symptoms. Just as in the state of highest ecstatic love, the highly inflamed emotional state is easily and clearly recognized.
